coredata.dc:title: Preparation and use of tyrosine-capped copper nanoclusters as fluorescent probe to determine rutin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Zhifeng
Chen, Siying
Ma, Xinru
Na, Dang
Zhao, Jie
Wu, Tianqi
Zhang, Caifeng
coredata.dc:description: For the first time, we developed a facile, green and low-cost strategy to prepare blue-green emitting fluorescence copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) by using tyrosine as a protecting agent and hydrazine hydrate as a reducing agent. The tyrosine-protected Cu NCs (Tyr-Cu NCs) were analyzed through fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The as-prepared Cu NCs was well dispersed and possessed good water solubility, excellent photostability and resistance to sodium chloride. The fluorescence intensity of Tyr-Cu NCs (around 480 nm under the excitation wavelength of 435 nm) was obviously quenched in the presence of rutin. According to this characteristic, a selective and sensitive fluorescence analytical method for the determination of rutin was established. The analytical method had a good linear relationship between F0/F and the concentrations of rutin (range from 0.1–60 μM) and the detection limit (LOD) was 0.016 μM. The possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching was caused by the IFE and static quenching. Meaningfully, the fluorescent probe was successfully employed to detect rutin in bovine serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Tyrosine
Quenching
Rutin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity of Tyr-Cu NCs as fluorescent sensor for the detection of rutin
Possible mechanism of the Tyr-Cu NCs for the determination of rutin
Practical application in bovine serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of Tyr-Cu NCs; (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of Tyr-Cu NCs under different excitation wavelength.
UV–vis absorption (A) and fluorescence emission (B) spectra of Tyr-Cu NCs and tyrosine.
TEM image of Tyr-Cu NCs (A); FT-IR spectrum of Tyr-Cu NCs and tyrosine (B); XPS spectrum of Tyr-Cu NCs (C) and Cu 2p (D).

The effect of different synthetic conditions on the fluorescence intensity of Tyr-Cu NCs. (A) the volume of tyrosine (from 5 to 11 mL); (B) the volume of hydrazine hydrate (from 0.5 to 3.0 mL); (C) reaction temperature (from 25 to 65 °C); (B) reaction time (from 1 to 10 h).
Effect of storage time (A), UV irradiation time (B), different concentrations of NaCl (C), pH (D) and solvent (1 mL Cu NCs+1 mL solvent) (E) on stability of Tyr-Cu NCs.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectrum of Tyr-Cu NCs with the increasing concentration (0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80 and 90 μM) of rutin at 435 nm excitation; (B) The calibration curve with rutin concentration from 0.1–1 μM; (C) The calibration curve with rutin concentration from 1–60 μM.

(A) Relative fluorescence intensities (F0/F) of Tyr-Cu NCs solution with addition of Na+, K+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Co2+, NH4 +, Cl−, CO3 2-, SO4 2-, AA, GSH and rutin (the concentration was 90 μM); (B) Ion interference of Tyr-Cu NCs in the reference.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of rutin, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of Tyr-Cu NCs; (B) The fluorescence lifetime of Tyr-Cu NCs and Tyr-Cu NCs-rutin system.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of tyrosine-Cu NCs.
A brief comparison of different analytical methods for the detection of rutin.
Results of the rutin recoveries in bovine serum samples.

head.ce:title: Preparation and use of tyrosine-capped copper nanoclusters as fluorescent probe to determine rutin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Humic Acid Engineering and Technology Research Center of Shanxi Province, Jinzhong 030619, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Humic Acid Engineering and Technology Research Center of Shanxi Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Humic Acid Engineering and Technology Research Center of Shanxi Province, Jinzhong 030619, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, for the first time, we have designed a fluorescent sensor based on tyrosine-Cu NCs for detection of rutin.

For the first time, we developed a facile, green and low-cost strategy to prepare blue-green emitting fluorescence copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) by using tyrosine as a protecting agent and hydrazine hydrate as a reducing agent. The tyrosine-protected Cu NCs (Tyr-Cu NCs) were analyzed through fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The as-prepared Cu NCs was well dispersed and possessed good water solubility, excellent photostability and resistance to sodium chloride. The fluorescence intensity of Tyr-Cu NCs (around 480 nm under the excitation wavelength of 435 nm) was obviously quenched in the presence of rutin. According to this characteristic, a selective and sensitive fluorescence analytical method for the determination of rutin was established. The analytical method had a good linear relationship between F0/F and the concentrations of rutin (range from 0.1–60 μM) and the detection limit (LOD) was 0.016 μM. The possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching was caused by the IFE and static quenching. Meaningfully, the fluorescent probe was successfully employed to detect rutin in bovine serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Rutin (Chart 1 ), flavonoids with antioxidant properties, is mainly extracted from sophora flower bud and widely used for the treatment of hyperglycemia, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, anti-oxidation, anti-adipogenic, chronic bronchitis and cancer [1–4]. At the same time, rutin is a natural sunscreen because of its strong absorption of ultraviolet and X-rays. Thence, rutin became an important component of sunscreen products. However, it can easily lead to adverse reactions in the case of excessive dosage. Therefore, the development of a rapid and sensitive analytical method for the detection of rutin is a necessary work. Some determination methods of high-performance liquid chromatography [5], UV–vis absorption spectroscopy [6], sequential injection analysis [7] and electrochemical methods [8–10] have been applied for the measurement of rutin. Although these methods showed the advantages of high sensitivity and accuracy, they also existed some drawbacks, like time consuming, high cost and tedious operation. Consequently, exploitation of rapid, convenient, low cost, time-saving, high selective and sensitive analytical methods for the measurement of rutin is very important.

Recently, a series of fluorescence sensors attracted more attention due to fast synthesis, easy operation, high sensitive and selective properties. Different types of fluorescent materials including quantum dots [11–13], carbon-based nanomaterials [14–16] and organic material [17,18] have been developed to analyze pollutant. However, these materials have obvious disadvantages, such as greater toxicity and harsh preparation conditions. Thus, the selective and sensitive determination of rutin by simple synthesis method is still a great challenge.

Metal nanoclusters (MNCs) have been employed to measure pollutant and harmful substance as a particular fluorescent material owing to its excellent optical properties, photostability and low toxicity [19–23]. Different kinds of metal nanoclusters including Au, Ag, Pt and Cu NCs [24–29] have been successfully prepared by using etch synthesis method [30], electrochemical synthesis method [31], microwave-assisted synthesis method [32], template-assisted synthesis method [33] and photoreduction synthesis method [34]. Compared to Ag and Au, Cu is an easily available and low-cost metal. Cu NCs have been used to determine Hg2+ [35], Pb2+ [36], Fe3+ [37], Cu2+ [38], H2S [39], iodide [40], nitrite ions [41], phosphate ion [42], dopamine [43], picric acid [44], 2, 4, 6-trinitrotoluene [45], nucleosides [46] and histamine [47]. It is well-known that Cu NCs are rarely used for the detection of rutin in practical application.

In this paper, for the first time, we synthesized blue-green emitting fluorescence copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) by using tyrosine as a protecting agent and hydrazine hydrate as a reducing agent at room temperature for 3 h (Scheme 1 ). The Tyr-Cu NCs were characterized by using UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, FT-IR, TEM and XPS. Moreover, the effect of storage time, UV irradiation time and NaCl concentration on the fluorescence intensity of the Tyr-Cu NCs were explored. At the same time, we investigated the feasibility of the Tyr-Cu NCs as fluorescent sensor for the detection of rutin.

The fluorescence characterization was recorded through F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo Japan) with the Ex/Em slits set at 10.0/10.0 nm and the scanning speed of 1200 nm min−1. The UV–vis measurements were performed on a Shimadzu 2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectrums were taken by a FTIR-8400S (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was acquired by using FEI Tecnai G2 F20 (United States). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization was carried out through ESCALAB 250XI (United States).

Cupric chloride (CuCl2, 99 %), hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O, 98 %) and tyrosine (Tyr, 99 %) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China. Sodium chloride (NaCl, 99 %), potassium chloride (KCl, 99 %), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 99 %), barium chloride (BaCl2, 99 %), lead chloride (PbCl2, 99 %), cobaltous chloride (CoCl2, 99 %), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl, 99 %), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, 99 %), sodium sulphate (Na2SO4, 99 %), ascorbic acid (AA, 99 %), glutathione (GSH, 99 %) and rutin (95 %) were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultra-pure water (18.25 M·cm−1) was purified using a Millipore Milli-Q.

The tyrosine-capped Cu NCs were prepared through a fast, green, one-step synthetic strategy. In a typical experiment, cupric chloride solution (1 mL, 0.01 M), sodium hydroxide solution of tyrosine (9 mL, 0.05 M) and hydrazine hydrate solution (2 mL, 0.1 M) were separately added into round-bottomed flask and fixed to 15 mL. Then the mixture solution was reacted under 25 °C for 3 h. The solutions gradually changed from colorless to dark yellow, implying successful synthesis of Cu NCs. Hereafter, the Cu NCs solutions were purified by using a dialysis membrane (MW: 1500D) at room temperature for 48 h. Finally, the Tyr-Cu NCs was stored under 4 °C for further use.

Firstly, 1 mL Tyr-Cu NCs and 1 mL H2O were gradually added into 5 mL centrifuge tube. Soon afterwards, different concentration of rutin solutions were dropped into the Tyr-Cu NCs solution (final concentrations of rutin were 0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80 and 90 μM). Then the mixture were reacted at room temperature for 1 min and employed to measure fluorescence intensity. Other references including Na+, K+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Co2+, NH4 +, Cl−, CO3 2-, SO4 2-, AA and GSH were used to explore selective experiments under the same conditions.

The bovine serum samples were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). To discuss the feasibility of Tyr-Cu NCs as a fluorescent probe for the detection of rutin, different concentrations of rutin were spiked into bovine serum samples. Then, the spiked samples were detected by using the fluorescent method.

Many characterization methods were used to explore the structure, composition and optical performance of Tyr-Cu NCs. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the excitation and emission wavelength of Tyr-Cu NCs were displayed around 435 and 480 nm, respectively. The Tyr-Cu NCs solution was dark yellow under visible light and displayed blue-green fluorescence under UV lamp (365 nm) (inset in Fig. 1A). Additionally, maximum emission peak position remained unchanged with the excitation wavelength increasing from 390 to 440 nm, suggesting the excitation-independent performance of Tyr-Cu NCs (Fig. 1B). At the same time, the fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption spectra of Tyr-Cu NCs and l-tyrosine were discussed. In Fig. 2 A, UV–vis absorption spectrum of Tyr-Cu NCs had an obvious peak around 425 nm. The only strong fluorescent signal of Tyr-Cu NCs in Fig. 2B revealed that the fluorescence originated from Cu NCs instead of tyrosine. Moreover, by using quinine sulphate as a standard, the fluorescent quantum yield of the prepared Tyr-Cu NCs was 2.2 %.

As shown in Fig. 3 A, Tyr-Cu NCs were well dispersed with an average diameter of 2 nm and not emerged aggregation. The surface structure of Tyr-Cu NCs were studied by FTIR spectroscopy. Some functional groups of tyrosine were found on the surface (Fig. 3B) which illustrated the successful synthesis of the protective layer of tyrosine. Moreover, the peak of 3250−3000 cm−1 was ascribed to the association peak of phenolic hydroxyl, and the association peak of phenolic hydroxyl disappeared, which indicated that the Cu-O bond may be formed. Five elements of C, O, N, Na and Cu were showed in XPS spectra (Fig. 3C). To further discuss the peak of Cu, the detailed peaks were displayed in Fig. 3D. Two binding energy peaks around 932.3 and 952.2 eV was corresponded with Cu° and Cu+ [48] of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 electrons from the XPS spectrum. Furthermore, the binding energy of Cu2+ at 942.0 eV was not observed, demonstrating that Cu2+ was absolutely reduced. The above analysis results indicated that the Tyr-Cu NCs were successfully synthesized by using a simple and innovative strategy.

To gain the maximum fluorescence intensity of Tyr-Cu NCs, a series of preparation conditions were carried out including the volume of tyrosine and hydrazine hydrate, reaction temperature and time. As displayed in Fig. 4 , optimal preparation conditions were selected as followed: the volume of tyrosine was 9 mL, the volume of hydrazine hydrate was 2 mL, the reaction temperature was 25 °C and the reaction time was 3 h, respectively. The Tyr-Cu NCs prepared under optimal experimental conditions were employed to detect rutin in subsequent study.

To our knowledge, the stability of Cu NCs is an important parameter in single-molecule imaging, sensing and catalysis. So we discussed the effect of storage time, UV irradiation time and concentrations of sodium chloride, pH and solvent (water, methanol, ethanol, isopropanol and acetone) on the fluorescent intensity of Tyr-Cu NCs. As presented in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity of Tyr-Cu NCs displayed very small change after 30 d storage, 60 min UV light irradiation, 0.25 M NaCl and in different solvent. However, the fluorescence intensity of Tyr-Cu NCs decreased, when the pH value was lower than 9. The decreased fluorescence was ascribed to the protonation of tyrosine at low pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of Tyr-Cu NCs as fluorescent sensor for the detection of rutin
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the Tyr-Cu NCs for the determination of rutin, effects of other references including Na+, K+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Co2+, NH4 +, Cl−, CO3 2-, SO4 2-, AA and GSH on the fluorescent intensity of Tyr-Cu NCs were recorded at concentrations of 90 μM. As revealed in Fig. 7 A, except rutin, there was no obvious fluorescence quenching phenomena were observed in the presence of other references. Competitive experiments were also conducted to study the influences of other references. The quenching caused by mixing rutin with the other references was observed (Fig. 7B). The selective and competitive experiment results indicated that the Tyr-Cu NCs had excellent selectivity to the determination of rutin as fluorescent sensor and great application potential in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the Tyr-Cu NCs for the determination of rutin
ce:section.ce:para:
Next, the possible mechanism of the Tyr-Cu NCs for the determination of rutin was evaluated by using fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime. Firstly, we analyzed the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of Tyr-Cu NCs and the UV–vis spectrum of rutin. As displayed in Fig. 8 A, a distinct absorption peak was discovered at 400 nm, and the excitation and emission wavelength of Tyr-Cu NCs were displayed around 435 and 480 nm, respectively. As we can see from the above results, the overlap phenomenon was observed between the UV–vis spectrum of the rutin and the excitation spectrum of Tyr-Cu NCs. Therefore, this could provide certain evidence for the inner filter effect (IFE) to take place [52]. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetimes of the Tyr-Cu NCs were recorded in the absence and presence of rutin to illustrate that the IFE mechanism was possible. As revealed in Fig. 8B, the lifetimes of Tyr-Cu NCs and Tyr-Cu NCs-rutin system were 3.288 and 3.254 ns, the fluctuation was less than 0.1 ns. Then, the fluorescence quenching mechanism could be attributed to the IFE rather than FRET. Meanwhile, the results implied that the possible quenching mechanism of Tyr-Cu NCs-rutin system was static quenching in the presence of rutin. To further verify the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence quenching experimental data were discussed by Stern-Volmer equation.
F 0 / F
=
1 + K S V [ Q ]
=
1 + K q τ 0 [ Q ]
Where, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of Tyr-Cu NCs in the absence and presence of rutin, respectively. K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. [Q] is the concentration of rutin, τ0 is the lifetime of the Tyr-Cu NCs (3.254 ns). As seen in Fig. 6B, K SV and K q are equal to 3.8 × 104 L·mol−1 and 1.17 × 1013 L mol−1 s−1, respectively. The K q value was greater than the maximum scatter collision quenching constant (2 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1), implying that the quenching mechanism was static quenching [41,53]. Therefore, after the addition of rutin, the quenching mechanism of Tyr-Cu NCs was the IFE and static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application in bovine serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The above fluorescence detection system was used to determine rutin in bovine serum samples. As shown in Table 2 , the determination results of rutin were very close to the spiked concentrations. The recovery results were also recorded and satisfactory recoveries of rutin were performed from 92.4 %–104.8 %. These results confirmed that this proposed fluorescence sensor was promising for the determination of rutin in bovine serum samples.

In summary, for the first time, we developed tyrosine protected copper nanoclusters (Tyr-Cu NCs) by using a simple and convenient chemical reduction route. The as-synthesized Cu NCs displayed good water solubility, excellent stability, strong blue-green fluorescence intensity and high dispersion. Interestingly, the fluorescence of Tyr-Cu NCs was selectively and sensitively quenched in the presence of rutin. With the increase of rutin concentrations, the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm gradually decreased. The linear detection range was 0.1–60 μM and the detection limit was 0.016 μM. The possible quenching mechanism of Tyr-Cu NCs was the IFE and static quenching in the presence of rutin. Ultimately, the Tyr-Cu NCs could be successfully applied for the detection of rutin as a fluorescent sensor in bovine serum samples.
I have made substantial contributions to the conception or design of the work; or the acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data for the work; AND
I have drafted the work or revised it critically for important intellectual content; AND I have approved the final version to be published; AND
I agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved.

All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript, including those who provided editing and writing assistance but who are not authors, are named in the Acknowledgments section of the manuscript and have given their written permission to be named. If the manuscript does not include Acknowledgments, it is because the authors have not received substantial contributions from nonauthors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Shanxi Applied Basic Research Project (Grant No. 201801D121257), the Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths (Grant No. 201801D221142), the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (Grant No. CXCY2004), the Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2020L0499), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Project for College Students in Shanxi Province (No. 2020486) and the Fund for Shanxi “1331 Project” Collaborative Innovation Center (Grant No. I018038).
